did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning approach integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Vision Transformers (ViT) for automatic melanoma diagnosis. The methodology includes preprocessing (denoising, thresholding), segmentation using the Sobel algorithm, and classification of dermoscopic images to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions, achieving 94.19% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and implementation of a hybrid deep learning model (CNN + Vision Transformer) for automatic melanoma diagnosis. It details a specific methodology including preprocessing, segmentation (comparing Sobel, Canny, Prewitt), and classification, along with quantitative results (accuracy, RMSE, PSNR, SSIM) on a dataset split 80-20. Although published in conference proceedings (ICUIS-2024), it reports new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Automatic Melanoma Diagnosis: Integrating Convolutional Neural Networks with Vision Transformers
journal: Proceedings of the Fourth International Conference on Ubiquitous Computing and Intelligent Information Systems (ICUIS-2024)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning approach for automatic melanoma diagnosis by integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Vision Transformers (ViT). The methodology involves preprocessing dermoscopic images using denoising and adaptive Gaussian thresholding, followed by segmentation using edge detection algorithms, where the Sobel algorithm was identified as the most effective. The segmented images were then classified using the combined CNN-ViT model to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The dataset was split into 80% for training and 20% for testing, with performance evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. The proposed hybrid model achieved a superior accuracy of 94.19%, outperforming standalone CNN and ViT models. The results demonstrate that combining Sobel segmentation with CNN-ViT classification enhances diagnostic precision and reliability for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for diagnosis and segmentation. The study focuses on detecting and classifying melanoma from dermoscopic images.
The negative class used in the binary classification task to distinguish from melanoma. The output layer produces probabilities for 'melanoma or non-melanoma classes'.

all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma or non-melanoma) using a hybrid CNN-ViT architecture. The output layer produces probabilities for these two classes via a Softmax function. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (94.19%), precision, recall, and F1 score.

The study employs edge detection algorithms (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt) for segmentation to identify lesion boundaries and extract features. Sobel was selected as the best algorithm based on RMSE, PSNR, and SSIM metrics.

datasets_used.name: Melanoma Skin Cancer dataset (Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from the Kaggle repository containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

use.training: 80% of the dataset (8,000 images) was used to train the hybrid CNN-ViT model.
use.testing: 20% of the dataset (2,000 images) was used for testing and performance evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the accuracy of image segmentation algorithms by measuring the average difference between detected edges and ground truth edges.
To measure signal fidelity in maintaining edge details during segmentation performance evaluation.
To measure the similarity between detected edges and ground truth edges for segmentation performance evaluation.
To evaluate the performance of the deep learning classification models (CNN, ViT, CNN-ViT).
To evaluate the performance of the deep learning classification models.
To evaluate the performance of the deep learning classification models.
To evaluate the performance of the deep learning classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between segmentation algorithms (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt) and classification models (CNN, ViT, CNN-ViT) were based on descriptive metrics (RMSE, PSNR, SSIM, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score) without statistical significance testing.

segmentation_results.note: Comparison of edge detection algorithms for segmentation performance.
segmentation_results.notes: Sobel was identified as the best algorithm for segmentation based on the highest SSIM and PSNR values, despite having a higher RMSE than Canny. The text states Sobel outperformed Canny and Prewitt in maintaining edge information with high fidelity.

classification_results.note: Performance of deep learning models on the test set (20% of dataset).
classification_results.notes: The hybrid CNN-ViT model achieved the highest accuracy (94.19%). Training accuracy peaked at approximately 84.95% initially and reached 93.99% by the end of 20 epochs. Validation loss showed a steady decline, indicating good generalization.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Vision Transformer (ViT)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained CNN and Vision Transformer models were implemented and then fine-tuned on segmented and denoised images.

backbone_components: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for low-level feature extraction (texture and color gradients)
Vision Transformer (ViT) for modeling global spatial relationships and long-range dependencies
architecture_details.integration_method: CNNs extract features from raw images, which are then fed into Vision Transformers for higher-level representation learning and decision making.
architecture_details.patch_size: Not explicitly reported (ViT processes images by dividing them into patches, but specific patch dimensions are not provided)

classification_or_output_head.layer_type: Global Average Pooling followed by Fully Connected Layer and Softmax Output

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filter (low-pass filter for noise reduction)
conclusions: The hybrid deep learning model integrating Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Vision Transformers (ViT) achieved a superior accuracy of 94.19%, outperforming standalone CNN (88.5%) and ViT (89.7%) models.

The Sobel edge detection algorithm was identified as the most effective segmentation method, demonstrating high fidelity in maintaining edge information with an SSIM of 0.9923 and PSNR of 26.98, outperforming Canny and Prewitt algorithms.
The proposed approach combining denoising, Sobel segmentation, and CNN-ViT classification enhances diagnostic precision and reliability for melanoma detection compared to conventional techniques.

The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, evidenced by a steady decline in validation loss and validation accuracy consistent with training accuracy, indicating a low degree of overfitting.

The integration of CNNs for low-level feature extraction and Vision Transformers for modeling global spatial relationships allows the model to capture subtle patterns like asymmetry and irregular borders, improving melanoma diagnosis.
Future work will focus on optimizing network depth, layer configurations, and attention mechanisms to further improve the strategy.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public dataset (Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer dataset) with 10,000 images, lacking external validation on independent clinical cohorts or diverse demographic groups, which limits generalizability.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between the hybrid CNN-ViT model and baseline models (CNN, ViT).

The dataset exhibits class imbalance with 5,500 benign (non-melanoma) and 4,500 malignant (melanoma) images; the paper does not report specific strategies used to mitigate this imbalance during training.
The paper does not report data augmentation techniques, raising potential concerns about overfitting despite claims of low overfitting based on training vs. validation accuracy trends.

The segmentation component uses traditional edge detection algorithms (Sobel, Canny, Prewitt) rather than deep learning-based segmentation, which may lack robustness compared to modern semantic segmentation networks for complex lesion boundaries.
The model was trained and tested on a fixed 80-20 split without cross-validation, which may lead to optimistic performance estimates dependent on the specific data partition.

The paper acknowledges the need for clinical validation studies and collaboration with dermatologists to assess practical performance, indicating current results are pre-clinical and not yet validated for real-world deployment.
Key hyperparameters such as the learning rate, optimizer type, and loss function are not explicitly reported, limiting reproducibility.
